Browse by flow

Switch interview mode

4 flows
High-level design interview prep HLD
Architecture, distributed systems trade-offs, and end-to-end system walkthroughs for classic system design rounds.
Low-level design interview prep LLD
Object modeling, extensible APIs, machine-coding structure, and follow-up handling for class-design rounds.
Data structures and algorithms interview prep DSA
Curated coding-round practice across general problem sets and company-specific question banks for array, tree, graph, DP, and mock-round prep.
AI Engineer learning path AI/ML
End-to-end AI/ML engineering from foundations through LLMs, RAG, agents, MLOps, and responsible AI for building production intelligent systems.

Curriculum → Data structures and algorithms interview prep → Core patterns for coding rounds → Recursion, backtracking, and search trees

Lesson 2 of 3

Recursion, backtracking, and search trees

Practice generating combinations, traversing choice trees, and pruning branches that cannot lead to a valid answer.

30-45 min DSA flow Core patterns for coding rounds 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Interviewers use these problems to evaluate structure: can you model decisions, stop conditions, and backtracking cleanup without losing track of the stack state?

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Pattern lens Name the core data structure choice before you code.2. Practice set Letter Combinations of a Phone Number · Medium · Backtracking3. Execution focus Walk one example manually before finalizing the code.

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Interviewers use these problems to evaluate structure: can you model decisions, stop conditions, and backtracking cleanup without losing track of the stack state? 2. Pattern lens Name the core data structure choice before you code.3. Practice set Letter Combinations of a Phone Number · Medium · Backtracking4. Execution focus Walk one example manually before finalizing the code.
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Interviewers use these problems to evaluate structure: can you model decisions, stop conditions, and backtracking cleanup without losing track of the stack state?

What to say first

  • Clarify constraints, input shape, and expected output before coding.
  • Choose the intended pattern and defend its complexity.
  • Narrate the invariant that keeps the solution correct.
2

Study section

Pattern lens

Use these prompts to rehearse state snapshots, choice ordering, pruning conditions, and the differences between exhaustive search, DFS, and memoized recursion.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Name the core data structure choice before you code.
  • State the target time and space complexity up front.
  • Use one invariant or pointer relationship to guide the implementation.
3

Study section

Practice set

Use this cluster to rehearse the same solving pattern across multiple prompts. Start with Letter Combinations of a Phone Number, Generate Parentheses, Permutations and keep your explanation focused on why the pattern applies.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Letter Combinations of a Phone Number · Medium · Backtracking
  • Generate Parentheses · Medium · Backtracking
  • Permutations · Medium · Backtracking
  • Subsets · Medium · Backtracking
  • Alien Dictionary · Meta · Trees and Graphs
  • Shortest Distance from All Buildings · Meta · Trees and Graphs
4

Study section

Execution focus

Write the base case first, then say what state is chosen, recursed on, and undone when control returns.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Walk one example manually before finalizing the code.
  • Call out the edge case that is most likely to break the first draft.
  • Finish by restating the complexity and one possible follow-up optimization.

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Clarify constraints, input shape, and expected output before coding.
  • Choose the intended pattern and defend its complexity.
  • Narrate the invariant that keeps the solution correct.
  • Test at least one edge case and one representative happy path aloud.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Jumping into code without naming the dominant pattern or invariant.
  • Optimizing too early instead of getting the first correct version working.
  • Ignoring duplicates, empty inputs, or off-by-one boundaries until the end.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • How do you know when a recursive choice should be undone during backtracking?
  • What pruning rule meaningfully shrinks the search tree for this problem family?
  • When should a recursion tree become memoized instead of enumerated?

Coding practice

DSA interview workspace

This lab only surfaces runnable DSA prompts from the lesson set and keeps the editor in a single-file interview format.

WASM runtime idle 6 runnable questions

Problem statement

Letter Combinations of a Phone Number

Medium Backtracking

Given a string containing digits from 2-9 inclusive, return all possible letter combinations that the number could represent. Return the answer in any order.

A mapping of digit to letters (just like on the telephone buttons) is given below. Note that 1 does not map to any letters.

 

Example 1:

Input: digits = "23"
Output: ["ad","ae","af","bd","be","bf","cd","ce","cf"]

Example 2:

Input: digits = ""
Output: []

Example 3:

Input: digits = "2"
Output: ["a","b","c"]

 

Constraints:

  • 0 <= digits.length <= 4
  • digits[i] is a digit in the range ['2', '9'].

Runtime model

Runs in-browser with the bundled Pyodide WebAssembly runtime.

Ln 1, Col 1 3 lines 8 words 0.1 KB python

Test case

Run result

Run the selected test case to compare your output against the expected result.
